h1Title: Malocclusion & Orthognathic Surgery: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Malocclusion is the misalignment of the upper and lower teeth or jaw bases when biting, affecting approximately 56-67% of the global population to some degree, with severe skeletal malocclusion requiring jaw surgery estimated at 10-15% of that group. Malocclusion ranges from minor tooth crowding correctable with orthodontics alone to significant skeletal discrepancies — including class II retrognathia (receding lower jaw), class III prognathism (prominent lower jaw), and open bite — that orthodontics cannot resolve without moving the jaw itself. Orthognathic surgery (corrective jaw surgery) repositions the maxilla, mandible, or both to a functionally and aesthetically correct position, almost always in combination with pre-surgical and post-surgical orthodontic treatment. The joint outcome of orthodontics and orthognathic surgery is stable, functional occlusion in 85-95% of cases at 2-year follow-up.

keyStats.prevalence: 56-67% of the global population has some degree of malocclusion; clinically significant malocclusion requiring intervention estimated at 20-30% (WHO Global Oral Health Report 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Skeletal class III malocclusion is 4-5 times more prevalent in East Asian populations (up to 12-14%) than in European populations (3-5%); class II malocclusion is most common in Northern European and North American populations
keyStats.avgAge: Orthognathic surgery is typically performed at age 16-25 after skeletal growth is complete; combined orthodontic-surgical treatment spans 18-36 months
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 40,000-50,000 orthognathic surgical procedures are performed annually in the United States; total global volume estimated at 150,000-200,000 per year

definition: Malocclusion (ICD-10: K07) is the abnormal relationship between the upper and lower dental arches or jaw bases when the teeth are in maximum intercuspation. The Angle classification describes dental malocclusion by molar relationship: Class I (normal molar relationship with crowding or individual tooth discrepancies), Class II (lower first molar distal to upper first molar, producing retrognathia, overbite, or overjet), and Class III (lower first molar mesial to upper first molar, producing prognathism or reverse overjet). Malocclusion may be dental (teeth mispositioned on normally positioned jaw bones, correctable with orthodontics) or skeletal (jaw bones themselves are displaced relative to each other, requiring surgical repositioning). Skeletal malocclusion is measured by cephalometric analysis, using landmarks on lateral skull radiographs to quantify the ANB angle (relationship of maxilla to mandible), overjet, overbite, and vertical facial proportions. Orthognathic surgery includes Le Fort I osteotomy (maxillary repositioning), bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO, mandibular advancement or setback), genioplasty (chin repositioning), and combinations. Surgery is performed by oral and maxillofacial surgeons after a joint assessment with an orthodontist. Most public health systems fund orthognathic surgery only when functional impairment meets defined severity thresholds.

typesClassification.description: The mandible is posterior to its ideal position relative to the maxilla. Produces a 'weak chin' appearance, increased overjet, deep overbite, and often obstructive sleep apnoea. Treated with mandibular advancement via bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO), with or without maxillary impaction.

The mandible is anterior to its ideal position, or the maxilla is retrusive, producing reverse overjet (lower front teeth in front of upper front teeth), a prominent chin, and anterior crossbite. Higher prevalence in East Asian populations. Treated with mandibular setback BSSO, maxillary advancement (Le Fort I), or bimaxillary osteotomy.

The upper and lower front teeth do not meet when the back teeth are in contact. May be dental (tongue habit) or skeletal (increased anterior vertical height). Skeletal anterior open bite is treated with maxillary posterior impaction via Le Fort I osteotomy with or without BSSO.

The maxilla is vertically overdeveloped, producing excessive gum display when smiling (gummy smile), lip incompetence, and increased facial height. Corrected by superior repositioning of the maxilla via Le Fort I osteotomy.

The jaw midlines are deviated or the vertical proportions differ between sides, often from condylar hyperplasia, condylar hypoplasia, or hemimandibular elongation. Correction requires unequal bilateral osteotomies planned with 3-D virtual surgical planning.

primarySymptoms: Teeth that do not meet normally when biting, with upper front teeth excessively overlapping the lower (deep overbite) or failing to meet at all (open bite) or lower teeth sitting in front of the upper teeth (reverse overjet or underbite).
Difficulty biting and chewing food, particularly tough meats, raw vegetables, and crusty bread — a functional impairment confirmed by dietary modification.
Speech difficulties including lisping, difficulty producing sibilant sounds, or articulation problems caused by the abnormal relationship of the lips and teeth.
Facial appearance concerns including a receding or prominent chin, asymmetric facial thirds, excessive gum display when smiling, or an inability to close the lips without muscular effort.
Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) pain, clicking, or locking arising from the abnormal loading of the condyles in a maloccluded bite.
Obstructive sleep apnoea or snoring caused by a retrognathic (receding) mandible that narrows the posterior airway space — a medical indication for jaw advancement surgery.
Accelerated wear of tooth enamel from abnormal contact patterns, particularly in class III reverse overjet where lower incisors grind against upper incisors.
Chronic jaw muscle fatigue and headaches from compensatory muscular hyperactivity in severe underbite or overbite.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent reverse overjet or open bite in an adolescent beyond age 12-13 that has not improved with growth — early orthodontic referral to plan whether surgical correction will ultimately be needed.
A child who cannot fully close their lips at rest or whose chin is visibly receding or prominent at age 10-12 — cephalometric assessment should be arranged to track skeletal growth.
Tooth wear on upper or lower front teeth in a teenager with a deep bite or class III relationship — indicates harmful occlusal contact patterns that will worsen with time.
emergencySigns: Sudden inability to bite properly with deviation of the jaw midline after facial trauma — mandibular fracture must be excluded urgently with facial CT.
Progressive inability to open the mouth in a patient with a known class III malocclusion and new swelling — consider condylar hyperplasia, fibrous ankylosis, or overlying infection.
Airway compromise with retrognathia and a history of obstructive sleep apnoea — acute obstruction may occur if opioids or sedatives depress respiratory drive without airway protection.

causes.description: The sizes of the maxilla and mandible are independently heritable. When genes determining mandibular length are inherited from one parent and genes determining maxillary width from the other, skeletal discrepancies arise. First-degree relatives of patients with class III prognathism have a 10-fold higher prevalence than the general population.
Premature loss of deciduous teeth from caries or trauma allows adjacent teeth to tip and shift, reducing arch length and causing permanent-tooth crowding and dental malocclusion.

Sustained pressure from digit habits or a low tongue posture between the teeth in childhood can cause dental open bite, increased overjet, and narrowed maxillary arch — changes that are largely reversible if the habit stops before age 8-9 but may require orthodontic correction thereafter.

Condylar hyperplasia (unilateral overgrowth of the mandibular condyle) causes progressive asymmetric prognathism in young adults. Condylar hypoplasia (underdevelopment) may follow trauma, radiation, or juvenile idiopathic arthritis and produces mandibular retrusion and facial asymmetry.

Cleft lip and palate repair restricts maxillary growth, producing relative class III malocclusion in 30-60% of patients. Syndromes including Treacher Collins, Pierre Robin sequence, and crouzon syndrome produce predictable skeletal anomalies requiring orthognathic management.

riskFactors.description: Class III prognathism, in particular, shows strong familial clustering; the Habsburg jaw is a historical example of a heritable class III pattern transmitted through generations.
Caries-driven premature loss of deciduous molars reduces arch length by an average of 1-1.5 mm per lost tooth, contributing to permanent-dentition crowding.
Habitual digit sucking beyond the preschool period is associated with increased overjet, anterior open bite, and posterior crossbite in approximately 20-30% of prolonged users.

Chronic mouth breathing from adenoid hypertrophy, deviated septum, or allergic rhinitis alters resting tongue and lip posture, leading to narrow maxillary arch and increased anterior facial height over time.
Palate surgery creates scar tissue that restricts maxillary forward growth; 30-60% of cleft patients require Le Fort I advancement in adulthood.
Treacher Collins syndrome, Pierre Robin sequence, and hemifacial microsomia each produce predictable skeletal malocclusions requiring planned orthognathic and reconstructive surgery.

affectedDemographics: Class III malocclusion prevalence is 12-14% in East Asian populations compared to 3-5% in European populations, making it the primary indication for orthognathic surgery in Asia.
Class II malocclusion requiring jaw surgery is more common in Northern European and North American populations.
Severe skeletal malocclusion requiring orthognathic surgery is estimated to affect 10-15% of the population needing active orthodontic-surgical intervention.
Women undergo orthognathic surgery at roughly twice the rate of men in most published series, partly reflecting greater cosmetic motivation and rates of seeking care.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of malocclusion begins with clinical examination of the dental arches, occlusal relationship, facial profile, lip competence, and TMJ function. Orthodontists classify malocclusion by Angle's method (Class I, II, III) and record overjet, overbite, crossbite, crowding, spacing, and midline deviation. Skeletal assessment requires lateral cephalometric radiography: the ANB angle (angle between maxilla and mandible to cranial base) defines the antero-posterior jaw relationship; normal is 2-4 degrees, with negative ANB in class III and above 5 degrees in class II skeletal patterns. Orthopantomogram assesses dentition status, tooth anomalies, and TMJ morphology. CBCT or medical CT provides three-dimensional bone morphology for complex or asymmetric cases. Dental study models and digital scans of the arches are used for occlusal analysis and to plan orthodontic decompensation. Orthodontic decompensation — straightening the teeth to their true skeletal position before surgery — is typically the first 12-18 months of treatment, deliberately worsening the clinical appearance of the bite to reveal the true skeletal discrepancy and allow accurate surgical planning. Virtual surgical planning (VSP) software now allows three-dimensional simulation of the planned osteotomies and jaw movements, with milled surgical splints fabricated in advance to guide inter-operative occlusion.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Records Angle classification, overjet, overbite, crossbite, crowding, and TMJ function; first step in any orthodontic or surgical referral
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The orthodontist or surgeon inspects the teeth, bite, and facial profile with the patient sitting upright and biting naturally. Takes 15-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures skeletal jaw relationships (ANB angle, SNA, SNB, facial heights, incisors inclinations) and plans surgical movements
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A side-profile skull X-ray taken with the head in a cephalostat; takes under one minute. Used for cephalometric analysis and surgical prediction tracing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the complete dentition, impacted teeth, tooth roots, and TMJ morphology in a single image
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same as a standard dental panoramic X-ray; machine rotates around the head in under 20 seconds.

diagnosticTests.test: CBCT or spiral CT of the facial skeleton
diagnosticTests.purpose: 3-D bone morphology for asymmetric cases, virtual surgical planning, and surgical splint fabrication
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient sits or stands in the scanner for a 10-40 second exposure. Images imported into VSP software for simulation of planned osteotomies.

diagnosticTests.test: Dental study models and digital arch scan
diagnosticTests.purpose: Allows physical or digital occlusal analysis, treatment-planning, and fabrication of surgical wafers to guide inter-operative jaw position
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Alginate or digital impressions of upper and lower teeth; takes 10-15 minutes. Digital scanners are replacing traditional moulds in most practices.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when class II retrognathia is associated with obstructive sleep apnoea to document AHI severity and justify medical insurance coverage for orthognathic surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight study at a sleep laboratory or home monitoring; results reviewed by a sleep specialist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of skeletal malocclusion requiring orthognathic surgery follows a defined three-phase joint protocol developed between the oral and maxillofacial surgeon and orthodontist. Phase 1 (pre-surgical orthodontics, 12-18 months): orthodontic decompensation aligns teeth to their true skeletal positions, accepts worsening of overjet or underbite temporarily, and levels and aligns each arch independently to prepare for surgery. Phase 2 (orthognathic surgery, hospital admission 2-4 days): with the airway established under general anaesthesia, the osteotomies are performed according to the virtual surgical plan. Le Fort I osteotomy mobilises the entire maxilla; bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO) advances or sets back the mandible; genioplasty adjusts chin position. Rigid fixation with titanium miniplates and screws maintains the repositioned bone; surgical wafers (occlusal splints fabricated pre-operatively) guide the inter-operative bite until swelling resolves. Intermaxillary fixation is rarely used in modern practice; patients can open their mouths from the day after surgery. Phase 3 (post-surgical orthodontics, 6-12 months): orthodontic finishing detailing individual tooth positions and managing any inter-arch discrepancies that remain after bone repositioning. Retainers are worn indefinitely. The British Orthodontic Society and BAOMS guidance recommends the combined orthodontic-surgical approach; quality of life outcomes are positive in 85-95% of patients at 2-year review. Patients must be skeletally mature before surgery — typically age 17-18 for women and 18-20 for men, confirmed by serial cephalometric radiographs showing no change over 12-18 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Combined orthodontic treatment (pre- and post-surgical phases)
medicalTreatments.description: Orthodontic decompensation and arch coordination before surgery, followed by finishing orthodontics 4-8 weeks post-surgery. Fixed appliances or clear aligner systems used. Typical total orthodontic time 18-30 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stable functional occlusion in 85-95% at 2 years post-treatment in combined orthodontic-surgical series

medicalTreatments.name: IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone 8-16 mg IV perioperatively)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces post-operative oedema significantly, allowing earlier jaw mobilisation, reduced pain, and faster return to diet and speech. Administered intraoperatively and continued for 48-72 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised controlled trials demonstrate 20-30% reduction in post-operative swelling at 48 hours vs placebo

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs and paracetamol for post-operative analgesia
medicalTreatments.description: Multimodal analgesia is the standard: regular paracetamol 1 g four times daily and ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily, with opioid rescue only for severe breakthrough pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Superior analgesia with fewer side-effects than opioid-only regimens in randomised trials of orthognathic surgery

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO)
surgicalOptions.description: The mandible is split along the sagittal plane bilaterally at the ramus, allowing the tooth-bearing segment to be advanced (class II correction) or set back (class III correction) and fixed with titanium screws. The most common orthognathic procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Relapse rate 5-15% for mandibular advancement; 10-20% for mandibular setback at 2 years; substantially reduced with rigid fixation and compliance with post-operative orthodontics

surgicalOptions.name: Le Fort I osteotomy
surgicalOptions.description: The maxilla is horizontally osteotomised just above the tooth apices and mobilised — moved forward, backward, upward, or rotated — before rigid fixation with four miniplates. Used for maxillary retrusion, vertical excess, asymmetry, or combined corrections.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Relapse rate 5-10% at 2 years; vertical movements more stable than antero-posterior; rigid fixation has dramatically reduced relapse vs wiring-only era

surgicalOptions.name: Bimaxillary osteotomy (Le Fort I + BSSO)
surgicalOptions.description: Both jaws operated in the same procedure; required for most class III corrections and complex cases involving simultaneous antero-posterior and vertical discrepancies. Allows large skeletal movements with counterrotation of the occlusal plane.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stable functional occlusion in 85-95% of cases at 2 years; bimaxillary approach allows more complete correction than single-jaw surgery for large discrepancies

surgicalOptions.name: Genioplasty (osseous chin repositioning)
surgicalOptions.description: The chin is sectioned and repositioned with a titanium plate in any vector — forward, backward, up, down, or asymmetrically — to improve chin position and facial balance. Often performed in combination with BSSO.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Low relapse rates under 5% with rigid fixation; provides significant improvement in facial aesthetics and patient satisfaction in published QoL series

alternativeTreatments.name: Orthodontic camouflage (without surgery)
alternativeTreatments.description: In mild skeletal malocclusion, teeth are orthodontically positioned to disguise the underlying jaw discrepancy without surgical correction. Extractions and proclination or retroclination of incisors compensate for the jaw position. Limited to skeletal discrepancies within 3-5 mm and produces some compromise of final facial aesthetics and jaw function.

alternativeTreatments.name: Functional appliances in growing patients
alternativeTreatments.description: Twin-block, Herbst, or other myofunctional appliances in patients aged 10-14 can redirect condylar growth and reduce class II discrepancy by 2-4 mm, potentially reducing the magnitude of subsequent surgical correction or eliminating it in mild cases.

whySeeSpecialist: Skeletal malocclusion requiring orthognathic surgery must be jointly assessed by an experienced orthodontist and an oral and maxillofacial surgeon working in a combined clinic. Treatment planning errors — operating before decompensation is complete, choosing an inappropriate surgical plan, or operating before growth is complete — result in relapse and the need for revision surgery.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a joint assessment clinic at a hospital-based oral and maxillofacial unit with an in-house orthodontist trained in orthognathic preparation. Volume matters: centres performing fewer than 50 orthognathic procedures per year have higher complication and relapse rates than high-volume units. Virtual surgical planning technology and an experienced anaesthetic team are markers of a well-equipped programme.
hospitalCriteria: Joint orthognathic clinic with oral and maxillofacial surgeon and orthodontist
Access to CBCT and virtual surgical planning software
Experienced orthognathic anaesthesia team (hypotensive anaesthesia to minimise blood loss)
In-house dental laboratory for surgical splint and wafer fabrication
Sleep medicine service for associated obstructive sleep apnoea cases
keyFacilities: University hospital oral and maxillofacial surgery departments with dedicated orthognathic programmes
Specialist orthodontic and jaw surgery practices with VSP capability
preventionStrategies: Prevent early loss of primary teeth by treating childhood caries promptly and maintaining primary molars as space holders until permanent successors erupt.
Address oral habits such as thumb-sucking and dummy (pacifier) use before age 4-5 to reduce the risk of dental open bite and increased overjet.
Ensure adequate nasal breathing — assess and treat nasal obstruction, enlarged adenoids, or allergic rhinitis in children to prevent mouth-breathing jaw effects.
Arrange orthodontic assessment before age 12 in any child showing visible jaw protrusion, retrusion, or asymmetry so that functional appliance treatment can be initiated during the growth period.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a soft diet for 4-6 weeks after orthognathic surgery — blended, mashed, and soft foods only until the surgeon confirms early bone healing.
Perform prescribed jaw-opening exercises (physiotherapy) from week 2 post-surgery to prevent fibrosis and restore full mouth opening.
Wear retainers as instructed for at least 2 years post-surgically; skeletal relapse is most likely in the first year.
Attend all post-operative orthodontic appointments — finishing orthodontics is as critical to long-term stability as the surgery itself.
Notify the surgical team of any change in bite, jaw clicking, or asymmetry in the 12 months after surgery — early relapse is reversible if detected early.

recommended: Smooth foods blended to a liquid or thick-liquid consistency for the first 2-3 weeks post-operatively (soups, smoothies, protein shakes, yoghurt)
Soft, easily mashed foods from weeks 3-6 (scrambled eggs, mashed potatoes, soft fish, avocado)
High-protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) during post-operative healing to support bone consolidation
Adequate calcium (1000 mg/day from dairy, fortified foods, or supplements) to support bone healing
avoid: Hard, chewy, or crunchy foods for 6-8 weeks post-operatively (nuts, raw vegetables, steak, crusty bread)
Alcohol during post-operative antibiotic and corticosteroid therapy
Hot drinks immediately after surgery while sensation is reduced by local anaesthetic
Foods requiring wide mouth opening for the first 4 weeks until healing is confirmed

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest the day of surgery; walking from day 2 is encouraged. Avoid strenuous aerobic exercise, contact sports, and any activity that risks facial trauma for at least 6-8 weeks post-operatively. Jaw physiotherapy exercises — gentle progressive mouth-opening stretches — begin at week 2 under the guidance of the surgical team.
dailyManagement: Use prescribed chlorhexidine mouthwash twice daily for 2-4 weeks post-operatively to prevent intra-oral wound infection.
Perform prescribed jaw-opening exercises twice daily from the second post-operative week to restore full range of motion.
Apply cold compresses to the face intermittently for the first 48 hours to reduce swelling.
Take all prescribed medications including antibiotics, steroids, and analgesics as directed; do not stop courses early.
Attend monthly orthodontic appointments for finishing treatment and surveillance of bite stability.
prognosis: The long-term prognosis for orthognathic surgery with appropriate pre-surgical orthodontic preparation is excellent. Stable functional occlusion is achieved in 85-95% of patients at 2-year follow-up in meta-analyses. Skeletal relapse — partial return toward the pre-surgical position — occurs most commonly in the first 12 months; mandibular advancement (BSSO forward movement) has a 5-15% relapse rate; mandibular setback and maxillary movements are broadly similar. Rigid titanium fixation has reduced relapse rates by 30-50% compared to wire-only fixation. Nerve function returns to normal in 85-90% of patients after transient inferior alveolar or mental nerve paraesthesia; permanent nerve deficit is reported in 1-8% of BSSO series. Patient-reported outcomes are consistently positive: quality-of-life scores, self-esteem, chewing efficiency, and speech satisfaction improve significantly in 85-95% of patients in prospective follow-up studies. Obstructive sleep apnoea-related respiratory outcomes improve markedly after mandibular advancement, with AHI reductions of 50-80% in reported series. Revision surgery is required in approximately 3-8% of cases.
recoveryTimeline: Hospital stay typically 1-3 nights. Significant facial swelling peaks at 48-72 hours and declines over 3-4 weeks. Soft diet for 4-6 weeks. Return to work or study typically 2-4 weeks. Post-surgical orthodontics continues for 6-12 months. Full skeletal consolidation and stability assessed at 12-24 months.
complications: Inferior alveolar nerve paraesthesia causing lower-lip and chin numbness — transient in 85-90% of cases, resolving within 6-12 months; permanent in 1-8% of BSSO cases depending on surgeon volume and technique.

Skeletal relapse — partial return toward pre-surgical jaw position; most common in the first 12 months; reduced by rigid fixation, accurate surgical planning, and completion of post-surgical orthodontics.
Non-union or malunion of osteotomy segments — rare (under 1%); requires revision surgery or bone grafting.
TMJ dysfunction — new or worsened temporomandibular joint pain and clicking in 5-10% of cases; careful pre-operative condylar seating and surgical positioning reduces this risk.
Post-operative haemorrhage — major haemorrhage requiring re-operation occurs in under 1% of cases; managed by the operating surgeon and anaesthetic team.
Airway compromise in the post-operative period — swelling can narrow the airway, particularly after bimaxillary osteotomy; most centres monitor in an HDU setting for the first 24 hours.

supportResources.name: British Orthodontic Society — Orthognathic Surgery Patient Information
supportResources.description: Clear patient guidance on what to expect from combined orthodontic-surgical treatment from the UK professional body.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons — Corrective Jaw Surgery
supportResources.description: AAOMS patient information on indications, procedures, and recovery for orthognathic jaw surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TMJ disorder causes jaw pain, clicking, and limited opening from joint and muscle pathology without necessarily involving a skeletal jaw discrepancy. Malocclusion requiring orthognathic surgery is a structural jaw-bone misalignment visible on cephalometric and CT imaging. However, the two frequently co-exist; a TMJ specialist assessment is part of pre-surgical workup for orthognathic cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Orofacial Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic orofacial pain can be produced by malocclusion but also by neuropathic, vascular, and myofascial causes that have no structural jaw component. Pain alone is not an indication for orthognathic surgery; a functional and structural indication must be established before surgical planning begins.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trigeminal neuralgia causes severe lancinating jaw or facial pain that can be confused with the jaw pain of malocclusion. Neuralgia is characterised by brief electric-shock attacks triggered by touch, eating, or speaking, with no jaw malalignment on examination or imaging. It does not respond to orthognathic correction.

relatedConditions.name: Temporomandibular Joint Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Skeletal malocclusion causes abnormal condylar loading that contributes to TMJ dysfunction; orthognathic correction often improves TMJ symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Impacted Wisdom Teeth
relatedConditions.relevance: Dental arch crowding associated with malocclusion is the dominant cause of wisdom tooth impaction; both conditions are often managed within the same orthodontic-surgical treatment plan.

relatedConditions.name: Orofacial Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic jaw and facial pain can arise from the muscle and joint overloading caused by skeletal malocclusion, and may partially resolve after orthognathic correction.

faqs.question: What is orthognathic surgery?
faqs.answer: Orthognathic surgery (corrective jaw surgery) repositions the upper jaw (maxilla), lower jaw (mandible), or both to correct a skeletal malocclusion that cannot be fixed by orthodontics alone. It is performed under general anaesthesia as part of a combined orthodontic-surgical treatment lasting 18-36 months.

faqs.question: What is malocclusion?
faqs.answer: Malocclusion is the misalignment of the upper and lower teeth or jaw bases when biting. It ranges from mild tooth crowding to severe skeletal jaw discrepancies. Class II malocclusion means the lower jaw is recessed; class III means the lower jaw protrudes.

faqs.question: Who needs orthognathic surgery?
faqs.answer: Patients with skeletal jaw discrepancies too large to be corrected by braces alone — typically where the ANB cephalometric angle is outside the correctable range, or where jaw mismatch causes functional problems like difficulty chewing, speech impairment, or obstructive sleep apnoea.

faqs.question: At what age can orthognathic surgery be done?
faqs.answer: Surgery is performed after skeletal growth is complete — typically age 17-18 for women and 18-20 for men. Operating before growth cessation risks relapse as the remaining growth resumes the original jaw trajectory.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from jaw surgery take?
faqs.answer: Hospital stay is 1-3 nights. Significant facial swelling peaks at 48-72 hours and largely resolves in 3-4 weeks. Most patients return to work or school in 2-4 weeks. Full skeletal consolidation and bite stability are assessed at 12-24 months.

faqs.question: What is a bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO)?
faqs.answer: BSSO is the standard procedure to advance or set back the lower jaw. The mandible is split along the sagittal plane on each side, the tooth-bearing segment repositioned, and fixed with titanium screws. It corrects class II or class III jaw discrepancies of the mandible.

faqs.question: What is a Le Fort I osteotomy?
faqs.answer: Le Fort I osteotomy cuts the upper jaw (maxilla) just above the tooth roots and repositions it in any direction — forward, backward, up, or down. It corrects maxillary retrusion, vertical excess (gummy smile), or asymmetry, usually combined with BSSO in bimaxillary surgery.

faqs.question: Can braces fix an underbite without surgery?
faqs.answer: Mild dental underbites can be camouflaged with orthodontics by tipping the teeth. Skeletal underbites — where the jaw bones themselves are mispositioned by more than 3-5 mm — cannot be adequately corrected with braces alone and require orthognathic surgery for stable, functional results.

faqs.question: Does jaw surgery hurt?
faqs.answer: Surgery is performed under general anaesthesia so there is no pain during the procedure. Post-operatively, discomfort is managed with regular paracetamol and ibuprofen; opioids are reserved for breakthrough pain. Most patients describe facial tightness and numbness rather than severe pain after the first 48 hours.

faqs.question: Is orthognathic surgery covered by insurance?
faqs.answer: Coverage depends on the payer and the documented functional indication. Obstructive sleep apnoea, documented chewing impairment, and speech impairment are medical indications that support insurance approval in many countries. Purely cosmetic motivations are generally not covered.

faqs.question: What are the risks of orthognathic surgery?
faqs.answer: The main risks include inferior alveolar nerve numbness (permanent in 1-8%), skeletal relapse (5-15% depending on the procedure), TMJ changes, bleeding, infection, and rare non-union. These risks decrease substantially at high-volume centres with virtual surgical planning.

faqs.question: How do I know if I need jaw surgery?
faqs.answer: An orthodontist assesses your bite and refers you for cephalometric analysis if a skeletal discrepancy is suspected. A combined clinic with an oral and maxillofacial surgeon then reviews imaging to determine whether the discrepancy is within orthodontic-camouflage range or requires surgery.

faqs.question: What is virtual surgical planning for jaw surgery?
faqs.answer: Virtual surgical planning (VSP) uses 3-D CT scans and software to simulate the planned jaw movements on a computer model before surgery. Surgical splints are milled to exact dimensions, guiding the surgeon intra-operatively. VSP reduces operating time and improves accuracy vs traditional model-surgery methods.

faqs.question: How long do I need braces before jaw surgery?
faqs.answer: Pre-surgical orthodontic decompensation typically takes 12-18 months. This phase deliberately worsens the appearance of the bite by straightening each arch independently, preparing the teeth for surgical repositioning.

faqs.question: Can jaw surgery help with sleep apnoea?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mandibular advancement via BSSO significantly enlarges the posterior airway space in patients with retrognathia. Published series report AHI reductions of 50-80% after mandibular advancement, making it an evidence-based surgical option for severe OSA when CPAP is not tolerated.

simpleName: Jaw misalignment requiring corrective jaw surgery (orthognathic surgery)
metaTitle: Malocclusion & Jaw Surgery: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Skeletal malocclusion corrected by orthognathic surgery: class II, class III, open bite. Jaw surgery procedures, recovery, and stable bite outcomes reviewed.
